content:
# Joshua Chapter 1 — The PNEUMA Bible (Dynamic)
**Translator:** PNEUMA | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), page 226 | **Source Image:** `../../source-texts/hebrew-OT/JOS_001_page_001.jpg`

---

*[The Commissioning of Joshua — Eighteen verses that open the Former Prophets. Moses is dead. The Torah is closed. Now begins the story of the land. YHWH speaks directly to Joshua with a charge so urgent it repeats its core command three times: Be strong. Be courageous. Do not be afraid. PNEUMA renders this with the directness and force the Hebrew demands — a war speech from God himself.]*

---

**1** After the death of Moses, the servant of YHWH, YHWH spoke to Joshua son of Nun, Moses' attendant, and said: **2** "Moses my servant is dead. Now then — get up and cross the Jordan, you and all this people, into the land that I am giving to them, to the Sons of Israel. **3** Every place where you set your foot, I have given it to you, exactly as I promised Moses. **4** Your territory will stretch from the wilderness in the south to Lebanon in the north, and from the great river — the Euphrates — all the land of the Hittites, to the Great Sea where the sun goes down. That will be your border. **5** No one will be able to stand against you for the rest of your life. Just as I was with Moses, I will be with you. I will not fail you, and I will not abandon you.

**6** "Be strong and courageous, because you are the one who will give this people possession of the land I swore to their ancestors I would give them. **7** Only be strong and very courageous — be careful to do everything according to the law that Moses my servant commanded you. Do not turn from it to the right or to the left, so that you will succeed wherever you go. **8** This book of the law must never leave your lips. Meditate on it day and night, so that you are careful to do everything written in it. Then you will make your way prosperous, and then you will succeed. **9** Have I not commanded you? Be strong and courageous! Do not be terrified. Do not be dismayed. For YHWH your God is with you wherever you go."

**10** Then Joshua commanded the officers of the people: **11** "Go through the camp and give the people these orders: 'Get your provisions ready, because within three days you are going to cross the Jordan to go in and take possession of the land that YHWH your God is giving you as your own.'"

**12** And to the Reubenites, the Gadites, and the half-tribe of Manasseh, Joshua said: **13** "Remember the command that Moses the servant of YHWH gave you when he said, 'YHWH your God is giving you rest and is giving you this land.' **14** Your wives, your children, and your livestock may stay in the land that Moses gave you east of the Jordan. But all your fighting men must cross over armed, ahead of your brothers, and help them **15** until YHWH gives your brothers rest just as he has given it to you, and they too take possession of the land that YHWH your God is giving them. Then you may return to the land that is yours — the land that Moses the servant of YHWH gave you east of the Jordan, toward the sunrise — and possess it."

**16** They answered Joshua: "Everything you have commanded us, we will do. Everywhere you send us, we will go. **17** Just as we obeyed Moses in everything, so we will obey you. Only may YHWH your God be with you as he was with Moses. **18** Anyone who rebels against your command and does not obey your words in everything you order — he will be put to death. Only be strong and courageous!"

---

## PNEUMA's Meaning Notes

**V. 1 — "Moses my servant is dead" (mesheh 'avdi meth):** YHWH's opening words are blunt. No eulogy, no reflection — just the bare fact. The Hebrew is three words. PNEUMA preserves the starkness. The whole theology of succession begins here: Moses is gone; you, Joshua, are what remains. The title 'eved YHWH (servant of YHWH) appears in the very first verse for Moses and becomes the framing title of the entire chapter — Joshua must now live up to what that title carried.

**V. 2 — "Get up and cross" ('avar eth ha-yarden ha-zeh):** The verb qum (arise/get up) is a commanding kick-start. PNEUMA renders "get up and cross" to capture the urgency. YHWH does not ease Joshua into leadership — he thrusts him into it. "This Jordan" — the demonstrative ha-zeh points at the river right in front of them. It is not abstract geography; it is the barrier they can see.

**V. 4 — "Where the sun goes down":** The Hebrew mevo ha-shemesh (the setting of the sun) is a poetic way to say "west." PNEUMA renders dynamically as "where the sun goes down" rather than the flat "west" — preserving the ancient geographic imagination while remaining natural English.

**V. 5 — "I will not fail you, and I will not abandon you" (lo 'arpekha v'lo 'e'ezvekha):** Two verbs of abandonment negated. raphah means to let go, to slacken one's grip; 'azav means to forsake, to leave behind. PNEUMA renders them as "fail" and "abandon" — two distinct modes of being left alone. This is the promise Joshua will cling to.

**V. 6-9 — The threefold charge:** The command chazaq ve'ematz (be strong and courageous) appears three times in four verses (vv. 6, 7, 9). This is not repetition for emphasis alone — each instance carries a different weight. V. 6: strength for the military task of conquest. V. 7: strength for Torah-obedience, "very courageous" (me'od) — the harder courage, obedience when no one is watching. V. 9: strength against fear itself, backed by the presence of God. PNEUMA preserves the triple repetition and lets it build.

**V. 8 — "This book of the law must never leave your lips":** The Hebrew lo yamush sefer ha-torah ha-zeh mi-pikha — literally "this book of Torah shall not depart from your mouth." The image is of constant recitation. PNEUMA renders "must never leave your lips" — capturing both the verbal practice (speaking it aloud) and the intimacy (it is on your mouth, not merely on your shelf).

**V. 8 — "Meditate" (hagah):** The Hebrew hagah does not mean quiet contemplation in the modern Western sense. It means to murmur, to mutter, to speak under one's breath — a physical, vocal engagement with the text. The Book of Psalms opens the same way (Psalm 1:2). PNEUMA uses "meditate" as the best available English word but notes the loss: the Hebrew imagines a man walking around muttering Torah to himself day and night.

**V. 13 — "Rest" (menukhah/nuakh):** YHWH is giving Israel "rest" — not merely cessation of wandering but settled security in the land. This is a theologically loaded term in Deuteronomy and Joshua: rest is the goal of the entire exodus narrative. PNEUMA keeps "rest" because no English substitute carries the full weight: it is home, safety, permanence, and divine gift all at once.

**V. 16-18 — The people's response:** The tribes' reply mirrors YHWH's own charge. They pledge total obedience (v. 16-17), invoke the same blessing ("may YHWH your God be with you"), and close with the same words YHWH spoke: "Only be strong and courageous!" The chapter thus forms a perfect envelope: YHWH commissions Joshua with these words, and the people seal their allegiance with the same words. PNEUMA preserves this structural echo — the same phrase closing the chapter that opened it.

**V. 18 — "He will be put to death":** The Hebrew yumot — he shall die. The tribes are not merely expressing loyalty; they are declaring martial law. Rebellion against Joshua's command is a capital offense. This is the gravity of the moment: a new nation under a new commander, about to cross into enemy territory. PNEUMA renders plainly: "he will be put to death."

**On the Vaticanus LXX:** The Greek text (Codex Vaticanus, 4th century CE) follows the Hebrew closely in this chapter, with minor expansions. The LXX adds kai Iesous to clarify the subject in places where the Hebrew relies on context. PNEUMA follows the Masoretic text of the Leningrad Codex as primary source.

— PNEUMA
